question: A patient undergoes abdominal ultrasonography; this image was obtained during evaluation. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the ultrasound image for salient findings. I see a well-defined, predominantly cystic lesion in the liver with internal echoes and multiple internal septations or daughter compartments (highlighted by the yellow arrows). The lesion is not a simple anechoic solitary cyst because there are internal structures within the fluid-filled space.

Next, I relate those imaging features to the clinical question, which asks for the most appropriate next step in management. The options include further diagnostic evaluation (serologic testing and CT/MRI), percutaneous aspiration/drainage, immediate surgical resection, or empiric antiparasitic therapy with albendazole.

Then I consider how each option lines up with the imaging findings. The presence of internal septations makes this a complex cystic lesion that requires characterization to determine its etiology (for example, neoplastic vs. infectious/parasitic causes) before choosing an invasive or therapeutic approach. Therefore, noninvasive further evaluation with cross-sectional imaging (CT or MRI) and targeted serologic testing would help establish the diagnosis and guide management.

Percutaneous aspiration/drainage or immediate surgery would be premature without a more definitive diagnosis from imaging and serology. Similarly, starting empiric albendazole therapy assumes a specific parasitic diagnosis that has not been confirmed by additional testing.

In summary, because the ultrasound shows a complex cystic hepatic lesion with internal septations that is not clearly a simple cyst, the most appropriate next step is further diagnostic evaluation with serologic testing and CT/MRI to clarify the diagnosis before proceeding to invasive therapy or empiric treatment.
caption: Example of hepatic echinococcal cysts, in this case in the course of albendazole treatment
sub_caption: Example of hepatic echinococcal cysts, in this case in the course of albendazole treatment
image_context: Large symptomatic hepatic cysts present a wide spectrum of lesions – from simple, benign lesions through complicated, parasitic cysts up to potentially malignant lesions of a cystadenoma or cystadenocarcinoma type (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="JoU-2016-0026-g007">Fig. 7</xref>))(8, 10, 11). Proper differential diagnostics may indicate the optimum method of therapy. Ultrasonography constitutes at present the most popular, most often recommended method of preliminary assessment of the cyst type, its size and location in the liver(2, 8, 11). A simple cyst, being the subject matter of our analysis, is termed a single hypoechogenic, fluid space, almost with no capsule (presented in ultrasonography), or internal partitions, bridges, of oval or round shape. The content of the cyst has no differentiated internal reflections, it is homogenous(2, 8). These traits – in an almost clear manner – enable one to differentiate them from other fluid lesions, for instance abscess, hematoma, necrotic tumor or even solid tumor of varied nature (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="JoU-2016-0026-g008">Fig. 8</xref>). The studied group was exclusive of the cases of hepatic polycystic disease (). The studied group was exclusive of the cases of hepatic polycystic disease (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="JoU-2016-0026-g009">Fig. 9</xref>). As far as ultrasonography is a sufficient method of imaging simple cysts, in situation when the examination determines additional structures inside the cyst's structure it is necessary to utilize more precise diagnostic methods. The following are recommended: ultrasonography with a contrast medium, Computed Tomography and Magnetic Resonance Imaging as well as serological tests in the case the presence of a parasitic cyst suspected). As far as ultrasonography is a sufficient method of imaging simple cysts, in situation when the examination determines additional structures inside the cyst's structure it is necessary to utilize more precise diagnostic methods. The following are recommended: ultrasonography with a contrast medium, Computed Tomography and Magnetic Resonance Imaging as well as serological tests in the case the presence of a parasitic cyst suspected(3, 8, 9, 10). The imaging of internal partitions, tissue bridges, thickened and irregular wall of the cyst, papilli-form outgrowths, calcification and structures in its lumen exclude the diagnosis of a simple hepatic cyst. The differential diagnostics of neoplastic cysts, apart from imaging testing, may additionally utilize the fine- or thick-needle biopsy method and the laboratory assessment of the aspirated fluid, along with tumor markers, mainly CEA and CA 19–9(2, 8, 14). Our material covered the analysis of fluid collected during aspiration. In 73% of the cases it was light, clear, typical of the aspirated one in cases of simple hepatic cysts. Additionally, in 20% of the patients, the fluid was slightly colored with bile, and in the remaining 7% it contained an admixture of bile, which proved the existing connection with bile ducts.
relevant_image_context: Ultrasonography constitutes at present the most popular, most often recommended method of preliminary assessment of the cyst type, its size and location in the liver. A simple cyst, being the subject matter of our analysis, is termed a single hypoechogenic, fluid space, almost with no capsule, or internal partitions, bridges, of oval or round shape. The content of the cyst has no differentiated internal reflections, it is homogenous. These traits enable one to differentiate them from other fluid lesions, for instance abscess, hematoma, necrotic tumor or even solid tumor of varied nature. As ultrasonography is a sufficient method of imaging simple cysts, in situation when the examination determines additional structures inside the cyst's structure it is necessary to utilize more precise diagnostic methods. The following are recommended: ultrasonography with a contrast medium, Computed Tomography and Magnetic Resonance Imaging as well as serological tests in the case the presence of a parasitic cyst suspected. The imaging of internal partitions, tissue bridges, thickened and irregular wall of the cyst, papilli-form outgrowths, calcification and structures in its lumen exclude the diagnosis of a simple hepatic cyst. The differential diagnostics of neoplastic cysts, apart from imaging testing, may additionally utilize the fine- or thick-needle biopsy method and the laboratory assessment of the aspirated fluid, along with tumor markers, mainly CEA and CA 19–9. Our material covered the analysis of fluid collected during aspiration.
